channel.title: The Hippie Scientist
channel.description: Independent research on psychoactive herbs, entheogens, and natural neurochemistry.

item.title: Extraction 101 — Skullcap
item.description: A practical extraction note for Scutellaria lateriflora (American skullcap), with emphasis on preparation choices and conservative use. Summary Skullcap is traditionally prepared as tea, tincture, or low-heat infusion.…

item.description: Daily notes on Passionflower with attention to active compounds like Beta-caryophyllene and safe practice. Highlights Context and traditional notes Quick pharmacology snapshot Practical prep and safety pointers Further…

item.title: Research Digest — Passionflower
item.description: Can’t sleep because your body is tired but your mind is still racing? Passionflower is often a better fit for that pattern than stronger “knock-you-out” options. What people misunderstand It’s not a hard sedative. Passi…

item.title: Rhodiola vs. Ashwagandha: Matching Adaptogens to Your Needs
item.description: Rhodiola (Rhodiola rosea) and ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) are two of the most popular adaptogens for stress resilience. While both herbs modulate the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, their personalities d…

item.title: Kava Safety: Kavalactones, Extraction, and Liver Risk
item.description: Kava (Piper methysticum) carries a dual reputation: a beloved ceremonial relaxant in the South Pacific and a controversial herb in Western regulatory circles. This article examines how kavalactone chemistry, extraction…

item.title: Cacao and Theobromine: A Gentle Stimulant Profile
item.description: Cacao ceremonies and morning cacao tonics are surging in popularity as people seek alternatives to coffee. Theobromine-rich cacao offers a smoother stimulant experience that supports focus, heart opening, and creative f…

item.title: Blue Lotus (Nymphaea) and Aporphines for Gentle Sedation
item.description: Blue lotus (Nymphaea caerulea) evokes images of ancient Egyptian rites and tranquil pools. Today, herbal enthusiasts infuse its petals in tea, wine, or tinctures for serene evenings. The key to understanding blue lotus…

item.title: Start Here: Building a Simple Nightly Herb Blend
item.description: Creating a nightly herb blend can transform bedtime from a scramble into a nourishing ritual. Instead of chasing complex formulas, start with a thoughtful foundation that aligns with your constitution, schedule, and sle…

item.title: Lion's Mane and BDNF: What the Evidence Shows
item.description: Lion’s mane mushroom (Hericium erinaceus) has become synonymous with brain health in modern herbal circles. Central to its reputation is brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), a signaling protein that supports synapt…

item.title: Yerba Mate vs. Green Tea: Balancing Caffeine and L-Theanine
item.description: Yerba mate and green tea share a reputation for steady energy, yet their chemistry and cultural contexts diverge. Mate gourds clink in South American plazas, while Japanese tea ceremonies unfold with precise choreograph…

item.title: Kanna (Sceletium) for Mood and Focus
item.description: Kanna, or Sceletium tortuosum, has long been chewed, smoked, and brewed across southern Africa for its calming lift. Today the succulent is finding a new audience among people who want gentle emotional buoyancy without…

item.title: Reishi for Sleep and Immunity: What the Studies Say
item.description: Reishi (Ganoderma lucidum) is revered as the “mushroom of immortality” in East Asian traditions. Modern research explores its beta-glucans, triterpenes, and polysaccharides for immune modulation and stress resilience. T…

item.title: Mugwort and Dreaming: Tradition Meets Evidence
item.description: Mugwort (Artemisia vulgaris) has woven itself into dream lore across Europe, Asia, and North America. Herbalists burn it as smudge, tuck sprigs under pillows, or sip it as bedtime tea to stimulate vivid dreams. But how…
